llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm exists: ai_assisted, which provided access to thirteen different LLMs. The reported statistics compare this treatment group against the control group with no AI assistance.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper reports effects per year of model progress (8% reduction overall, 10.7% for non-agentic, 4.8% for agentic) and per tenfold increase in training compute (6% reduction, coefficient -0.0608). These are scaling relationships rather than direct AI vs. control comparisons. The primary statistic extracted is the overall 8% reduction per year of model progress (p≈0.04). The regression coefficient of -0.0608 for the compute scaling is also provided, but without a standard error (SE not reported in the verbatim quote). Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms data. It is unclear whether the outcome was log-transformed in the regression; the coefficient -0.0608 corresponds to ~6% reduction, suggesting possible log transformation, but this is not explicitly stated. The reported_percent_change reflects the 8% reduction per year as -0.08.
source_quotes: Moving one year forward in model release date is associated with roughly an 8% reduction in time taken to complete a task as shown in Figure 5 below (p≈0.04, full regression results and a breakdown of the scaling law by profession can be found in Table 9 and Figure A2 of Appendix A respectively).
For non-agentic tasks, the scaling curve is steep: the estimated coefficient implies that task completion time falls by approximately 10.7% per year of frontier model progress (p <0.05).
For agentic tasks, while the sign remains positive, the slope is considerably flatter (approximately 4.8% reduction per year) and statistically indistinguishable from zero in this sample.
The regression analysis reveals a log-linear relationship where a ten-fold increase in training compute corresponds to approximately a 6% reduction in task completion time (p <0.31).

Sample sizes (n) are not provided in the arms data.
